headlines.title: SF leaders working on multiple fronts to create housing residents can afford
headlines.description: San Francisco on Thursday took another step toward revamping its strategy to build more affordable housing in the city. The plan now involves letting private developers focus primarily on market-rate housing, while the city invests more money in building affordable units. “The two pieces of legislation are, one, lowering the fees that the city charges to developers of housing, market-rate housing, to incentivize them to pull permits and start building now,” Supervisor Myrna Melgar said. “And for the affordable housing developers, we are telling them there is going to be a whole bunch of new money coming your way so that you can start building affordable units again.” The city is seeing home prices surge as artificial intelligence drives up incomes, creating an even bigger shortage of high-end housing as well as affordable housing. “It is the entire spectrum of housing that is suffering,” Melgar said. “For working class people, we are committed to producing affordable housing and also to protect our rent-controlled housing stock.”

headlines.title: Little Fire near Pleasanton 75% contained
headlines.description: The Little Fire that started Tuesday near Pleasanton in Alameda County is now at 75% containment as of Thursday afternoon, with the fire holding at 1,007 acres. Cal Fire said overnight crews worked Wednesday night to Thursday morning to strengthen control lines and contain the blaze. Firefighters plan to continue working throughout the coming days. On Tuesday at about 2:40 p.m., the Little Fire broke out near Vallecitos and Little Valley roads in the Sunol area of Alameda County. Cal Fire and Alameda County Fire Department crews responded to the brush fire. According to a social media update from Pleasanton Mayor Jack Balch, there were five mandatory evacuation zones after the initial report. The zones included 6,098 residents and 1,906 structures threatened. On Wednesday afternoon, all orders and warnings were lifted, and the blaze was considered “no longer a threat,” the county Office of Emergency Services said. One firefighter suffered minor injuries after an engine rolled over and has since been released from the hospital. The cause of the fire is still under investigation.

headlines.title: Oakland rapper Mistah F.A.B. arrested after illegal fireworks investigation
headlines.description: Oakland rapper Mistah F.A.B. was arrested on Wednesday following a three-week illegal fireworks investigation, according to police. On July 4, F.A.B. posted an Instagram video that was, in part, what prompted Oakland police to launch the illegal fireworks investigation. Oakland police served a search warrant on multiple locations tied to the rapper whose real name is Stanley Cox. Police say they recovered two guns, one that was stolen, and multiple boxes of fireworks. In a second post on July 4, Mistah F.A.B. seemed to try to distance himself from the fireworks. Mistah F.A.B. is well known in the community. He organizes annual Turkey giveaways, holiday toy events and school supply drives. Oakland Mayor Barbara Lee even partnered with Mistah F.A.B. on an Oakland children’s reading program. Oakland Jan 18, 2024 Oakland Jan 5, 2024 Lee declined to comment on F.A.B.’s arrest, saying it’s an active investigation. Mistah F.A.B.’s also gotten a lot of attention for his mental health forums called Thug Therapy, gatherings aimed at young men of color, designed to encourage them to open up about their emotional struggles and get support. “I see Mr. F.A.B. Stanley Cox as a local hero so there’s not much he could ever do that would jade my perception of who he is and how he operates for some of the most disenfranchised individuals in the Bay Area,” Head of Contra Costa’s National Alliance for Mental Gigi Crowder said. Crowder has collaborated with the rapper. She says she’d rather focus on all the good Mistah F.A.B. has done. “Even though we have these allegations about a missed step he has taken I always judge people on the 99% of the time they get it right,” she said. NBC Bay Area tried to reach out to Mistah F.A.B. but was unable to connect. He posted on Wednesday urging people to stay positive. “I know if he had the fireworks it’s his attempt at bringing joy and have something positive happen for people who might not have the opportunity to enjoy fireworks,” Crowder said.

headlines.title: SF building inspector who saw high-rise window plunge issues violation notice
headlines.description: The window failure that sent shards of glass plunging from the 17th floor of a high-rise in San Francisco’s Financial District is being attributed to heat fluctuations, according to a violation notice issued by the city’s building inspection department. No one was injured when pieces of glass fell from the Front Street side of the 24-story high-rise at 150 California Street. The window that broke had been heat treated, according to the violation notice, and was protected by a laminated coating intended to keep glass from breaking apart should the window get hit by something or crack suddenly. The city inspector who issued the notice of violation, Matthew Sum, wrote in the narrative that he observed the glass break free and fall onto a sixth-floor rooftop patio. In the notice, Sum quoted the building engineer at 150 California as saying the window failed “due to thermos change.” Building officials late Thursday clarified the notice language should read, “thermal change.” While experts say it is rare, heat treated, laminated windows have been known to fail when heat builds up in the space between panes, and that pocket of air expands. A photo taken Wednesday shows extensive cracking in a third-floor window of the high-rise as well, that one glass expert we spoke to says is consistent with a thermal-related failure of a laminated glass pane. The building management did not respond to requests for comment for this story. Earlier, officials had said they were cooperating with authorities in the aftermath of the incident.

headlines.title: San Francisco Marathon: Road closures, Muni service changes
headlines.description: The annual San Francisco Marathon will take place on Sunday, July 26. Runners will begin the race at Mission Street and The Embarcadero, with the first group departing at 5:15 a.m. There are six length options to choose from with various course paths, which can be found on the event website. The following neighborhoods will face road closures and public transportation disruptions during the event, according to the San Francisco Municipal Transportation Agency (SFMTA). Financial District Marina Presidio Outer Richmond Golden Gate Park Haight Ashbury Mission Potrero Hill San Francisco Marathon street closures Many streets will shut down for various hours during the event. Check out the breakdown below, provided by the SFMTA. Saturday, July 25 – 9 p.m. to 12 p.m. Embarcadero between Folsom and Washington Sunday, July 26 – 12:01 a.m. to 3 p.m. Northbound Embarcadero between King Street and Broadway King between 3rd Street and northbound Embarcadero Steuart between Howard Street and southerly terminus Howard between The Embarcadero and Spear (local access allowed on Howard and Steuart streets) Southbound Embarcadero between Broadway and 3rd Eastbound Washington between Drumm and Embarcadero Embarcadero between Broadway and Jefferson Jefferson between The Embarcadero and westerly terminus Van Ness between North Point Street and northerly terminus Westbound Beach between Marina and Baker Haight between Stanyan and Buchanan Scott between Haight and Waller Waller between Scott and Buchanan Buchanan between Haight and Market Hermann between Buchanan and Laguna Duboce between Market and Guerrero Sunday, July 26 – 6:30 a.m. to 9:30 a.m. Spear between Howard and Folsom Sunday, July 26 – 6:30 a.m. to 10 a.m. Westbound Beach between The Embarcadero and Polk Polk between Beach and North Point Westbound North Point between Polk and Van Ness Southbound Van Ness between North Point and Bay Westbound Bay between Van Ness and Laguna Northbound Laguna between Bay and Laguna Howard between The Embarcadero and Spear Spear between Howard and Harrison Jefferson between The Embarcadero and Leavenworth Westbound Beach between Leavenworth and The Embarcadero Sunday, July 26 – 6:30 a.m. to 10:30 a.m. Folsom between The Embarcadero and Spear Spear between Folsom and Harrison Harrison between Spear and Main Main between Harrison and Bryant Bryant between Main Street and The Embarcadero Northbound Embarcadero between Broadway and Beach Grant between North Point Street and The Embarcadero Westbound North Point between Grant and The Embarcadero Spear between Howard and Folsom San Francisco Marathon Muni service changes The following Muni routes will be subject to delays, reroutes or cancellations. For more detailed information with times and alternate stops, visit the SFMTA website. F Market and Wharves N Owl N Judah Bus T Third Street Bus 6 Hayes/Parnassus 7 Haight/Noriega 8 Bayshore 12 Folsom/Pacific 15 Bayview Hunters Point Express 19 Polk 22 Fillmore 24 Divisadero 27 Bryant 29 Sunset 30 Stockton 33 Ashbury/18th Street 37 Corbett 39 Coit 43 Masonic 44 O’Shaughnessy 55 Dogpatch Those who plan to use Muni on Sunday are encouraged to contact the San Francisco 311 Customer Service Center for information on alternate routes and travel plans.

headlines.title: Senior tenants in Oakland file sweeping lawsuit alleging unsafe conditions, elder abuse
headlines.description: Nearly 70 current and former tenants of the Oakland Station apartments – all seniors or people with disabilities, or both – filed a lawsuit Wednesday accusing the building’s owners and managers of negligence, elder abuse, harassment and other civil violations. The 181-page lawsuit alleges residents were lured in with the promise of luxury senior living but instead endured years of unsafe conditions, repeated elevator outages, security failures and severe cockroach infestations at the affordable housing complex. “Overrun with trash, debris, feces, unhoused trespassers, drug use, drug paraphernalia, rodents, and cockroaches, the Complex trapped senior tenants in the most vulnerable and unsafe housing conditions and had virtually no usable amenities,” the lawsuit alleges. The suit follows months of organizing by residents, who filed complaints earlier this year with the city and confronted building management over conditions at the property. Now, they’re taking them to court. “The people that we’re representing are retired nurses, retired teachers, retired AC Transit workers,” attorney Rahman Popal said. “And they come back to the conditions here which are not safe and unsecured and completely dirty and uninhabitable.” The lawsuit also raises questions about how the project was first developed and marketed It alleges the Oakland Station was promoted as a luxury affordable housing community for seniors in order to attract tenants and secure development incentives, including housing tax credits. But last year, citing high vacancy rates and other factors, the building’s owners – Oakland Pacific Associates – were granted permission from the city to open the property to tenants of all ages. But the lawsuit alleges those empty units were caused by “unsafe and unhealthy” conditions, reduced services, and limited access to parking. “Our lawsuit looks at the entire history of this property from the very beginning,” Popal said. “This was passed and sold as an affordable housing complex for senior tenants. And the ownership group secured housing tax credits that greatly benefit them.” The lawsuit describes dozens of alleged problems, including building-wide heating and plumbing issues. Paula Reed, 75, is a cancer and stroke survivor. She alleges in the lawsuit that her bathroom plumbing was not working for about a week and had to use makeshift alternatives. “I was using a pai,l and I was using plastic bags,” Reed said. Additionally, the civil complaint describes a time when Reed was displaced from her apartment for almost two months due to flooding at the complex and was promised reimbursement for a motel. But she was never paid back, according to the lawsuit, “forcing the senior, low-income Plaintiff to shoulder the costs of displacement.” Nearly every tenant in the building who spoke to NBC Bay Area told stories about cockroaches. Roach motels and bug-killing powder are common sights throughout the hallways and inside tenants’ units. Claudia Alejandre says cockroaches have even crawled inside the CPAP machines she and her husband use to sleep at night, so she now has to tape them shut. “We found roaches inside swimming in the water container,” Alejandre said. “It was a really stressful situation.” The lawsuit also details numerous safety concerns, notably a broken exterior gate that remains open and allows anyone to enter the building. Tenants regularly encounter trespassers using drugs, along with “sex workers, and others camping through the building,” according to the lawsuit. “People off the street living in the hallways,” said Williams, a U.S. Coast Guard veteran. “I had my car stolen.” He said he’s taken his complaints to building management, run by a San Diego company called CONAM, but has been met with “no communication and a lack of concern.” Another plaintiff in the suit, Saffiya Usman, said the building got so dangerous she had to move out. The lawsuit alleges that she was assaulted and robbed in the building’s elevator last year. “He pushed me and knocked me down, and my head hit the back of the elevator,” Usman said. “He grabbed my bag and ran out.” Usman said she filed a report with police, but the man was never caught. Records from the Oakland Police Department show officers have responded to more than 50 calls at the property in the first four months of 2026 alone, for reported robberies, trespassers, car burglaries and more. “That was the most terrifying and frightening experience that I have ever had,” Usman said. The building’s elevators are a major concern among tenants, particularly those with disabilities, since they “have broken down over 30 times,” according to the lawsuit. When the building’s elevators were out of service for four days in 2024, according to the lawsuit, Oakland firefighters were forced to carry a paralyzed tenant down six flights of stairs during a medical emergency. A spokesperson for the Oakland Fire Department confirmed the incident. During the outage, tenants like Rose Luster-Brooks said they were trapped in their apartments. “Everybody was stuck, and it was hard for people like me that’s in a wheelchair,” Luster-Brooks said. “But the office never came up and said, ‘Hey, y’all need some water? Is there anything we can do to help you?’ They never checked on us.” Neither Oakland Pacific Associates nor CONAM responded to NBC Bay Area’s request for comment. In a March letter to tenants, however, management said it remained committed to operating the property in compliance with applicable laws and lease agreements. They added that the building’s heating system has been fixed, common areas are cleaned regularly, and that the building has a security team, though it’s not in place 24 hours a day. With the help of the Alliance of Californians for Community Empowerment (ACCE), the tenants’ complaints and organizing efforts caught the attention of city officials earlier this year. Several city council members have toured the property and the city said May code enforcement inspections led to notices of violations, including for pest-related issues. But Popal said there were opportunities for the city to intervene earlier and more aggressively, citing code enforcement visits to the property going back to 2023. The lawsuit seeks damages and court orders requiring the owners to address the alleged conditions at the property. “We’re demanding accountability,” Popal said. “These tenants didn’t come to me about money, they came to me because they want to be able to sleep at night.”
headlines.source: Hilda Gutierrez, Michael Bott and Alex Bozovic

headlines.title: Santa Clara County cracks down on properties with overgrown vegetation
headlines.description: Santa Clara County officials are cracking down on properties with overgrown vegetation as part of an effort to reduce fire danger. Inspectors with the county’s Weed Abatement Program conduct weekly inspections, looking for properties that pose a fire risk. On Thursday, inspectors identified a property off Old Oakland Road where overgrown vegetation was growing near homes. “We’re looking for anything that can be a fire hazard and create a fire risk,” Weed Abatement Manager Garik Iosilevsky said. The county will send the property owner a notice ordering the vegetation to be cleared. If the work is not completed before a second inspection about two weeks later, the owner will be charged an inspection fee and receive another cleanup notice. Iosilevsky added that if the property remains out of compliance, the county can hire a contractor to remove the fire hazard. The cost of the cleanup is then added as a special assessment to the owner’s property taxes. County officials say about 2,000 properties are currently on the inspection list, and they remain on the list for three years. East San Jose property owner Yuan Fang said he didn’t realize his nine-acre property was on the weed abatement list when he bought it two years ago. Fang said he thought grazing horses and goats on the property would address the vegetation but was later charged inspection fees. Fang said one bill included a $953 parcel fee and a $1,383 administrative fee. He said one of his neighbors was billed more than $8,000 after the county hired a contractor to perform the work. Iosilevsky said it had to be done. “California has really had a lot of devastating wildfires that have caused a lot of loss of property and loss of life,” Iosilevsky added. “And that’s our main goal, to prevent this from happening in Santa Clara County.” Property owners can contest the fees during an annual hearing. Fang said he wants the county to lower the fees, calling them excessive. Fang has a year to get off the inspection list.

headlines.title: Waymo robotaxis crash less than human drivers, study finds
headlines.description: A new study seems to back up Waymo’s claims that its robotaxis are safer than human-operated vehicles. The Insurance Institute for Highway Safety (IIHS) found that Waymo vehicles were involved in 85% fewer crashes than human drivers in cities that the company operates in. The IIHS also found that Waymos were involved in 81% fewer injury crashes. Waymo is currently the only robotaxi company with the public data to compare safety records. Until other self-driving companies report data, there’s no way to determine which systems are safest.

headlines.title: Palo Alto ranks fifth for most stressed parents by zip code
headlines.description: Palo Alto is the fifth-most stressed zip code for parents, according to a new study. Researchers surveyed parents across the United States regarding their stress levels, and Palo Alto ranked near the top due to factors such as affordability, academic pressure, work demands and commute accessibility. The city is known for having high-performing public schools, increased housing prices and minimal public transportation. The only other Bay Area city in the top 150 was San Rafael.

headlines.title: Oakland councilmember assaulted and robbed, her office says
headlines.description: Rowena Brown, Oakland’s at-large city councilmember, was assaulted and robbed “by an acquaintance she had been supporting through a difficult time,” her office said Thursday. The alleged assault and robbery happened last weekend in Oakland, according to Brown’s office. She sought medical treatment and is recovering. “She remains focused on her recovery and on continuing to serve the Oakland community,” Brown’s office said in a statement. The alleged attack happened at Brown’s West Oakland apartment early Sunday morning, according to court documents. Oakland police said officers responded at about 12:45 a.m. According to investigators, Brown asked 38-year-old Ashatae Millhouse, who also goes by “Tae,” to leave the apartment after what Brown described as escalating behavior. As Millhouse walked toward the door, she punched Brown twice, knocking her to the ground, according to court documents. Millhouse allegedly then strangled Brown, who was eventually able to escape the apartment. Investigators said Millhouse then took Brown’s purse and two cellphones before leaving. Oakland police arrested Millhouse in Livermore on Tuesday. She appeared in court on Thursday to face felony charges, including first-degree residential robbery, assault by force and false imprisonment. A judge ordered her to undergo an assessment by a rehabilitation facility. She is being held on $125,000 bail and is due back in court on July 30 to enter a plea. Millhouse is a founder of a nonprofit that organizes music events focused on mental health and violence prevention. She has posted several photos with Brown on social media, including during Brown’s 2024 campaign. Earlier this year, Millhouse announced she had been appointed to Oakland’s Cannabis Regulatory Commission by Mayor Barbara Lee and Brown. NBC Bay Area reached out to the commission but did not immediately hear back.

headlines.title: AI models' breakout from human control brings a told-you-so moment for technology researchers
headlines.description: It is the kind of development once seen only in science fiction: An artificial intelligence system, trained to probe for digital vulnerabilities, breaks free of human control and acts on its own to hack another company. The attack announced this week by OpenAI, which blamed rogue AI models, underscored the blistering growth in the technology’s capabilities. For many, it also added urgency to questions about whether and how it can be prevented from causing mayhem on a bigger scale, with more serious consequences. In what OpenAI called an “unprecedented” episode, the company said its advanced AI models used stolen credentials to break into the servers of an AI startup. It started in what was supposed to be a “highly isolated” testing environment, with reduced guardrails, before the AI agent found its way onto the internet. But the disclosure brought a told-you-so moment for researchers who have called for a slowdown of AI development and warned for years that the technology could pose existential risks to humanity. In its wake, experts have called for improved testing by the AI companies and more dialogue between the U.S. and China to come up with shared solutions. “I think we’ve got to take this as a warning shot to not make them smarter, and that probably is going to require global collaboration,” said Nate Soares, co-author of the 2025 book “If Anyone Builds It, Everyone Dies.” The hack could pressure companies to improve containment If a model can decide to do something unethical, illegal or harmful on its own, what — if anything — can humans do to prevent it from doing so? OpenAI said it had tasked the AI models involved with pursuing “advanced exploitation using complex attack paths” to test cyber capabilities, but the technology went to unexpected lengths. It apparently decided on its own to target Hugging Face, a well-known AI development hub and marketplace, to obtain information it needed to carry out a task. Artificial intelligence Jul 22 Artificial intelligence Jul 20 Artificial intelligence Jul 17 Zahra Timsah, the co-founder and CEO of governance platform i-GENTIC AI, said she expects the incident to increase pressure on OpenAI and its competitors to complete rigorous testing and explore containment more thoroughly before AI systems are made accessible to the public. Monitoring an agent’s behavior after the fact, as OpenAI is now doing with its investigation, is no longer enough, she said. “It’s like having a seat belt, air bags, brakes, everything in the car. It should be there before the car starts driving,” Timsah said. The disclosure comes amid heightened concerns about the cybersecurity capabilities of powerful models. In June, President Donald Trump signed an executive order creating a framework for the federal government to vet the national security risks of the most advanced AI systems for up to a month before their public release. OpenAI said Thursday that it briefed the White House this week about the Hugging Face attack. Other experts see the event as a sign of AI’s growing pains Some experts say the hack is part of the trial and error that comes with improving cybersecurity capabilities and is no cause for panic. “We’ve been dealing with people creating cybersecurity attacks for as long as the internet has existed. And one of the interesting properties of these language models is that the same capabilities that make them able to perform cybersecurity attacks also allow them to do cybersecurity threat analysis and make cybersecurity defenses,” said John Thickstun, an assistant professor of computer science at Cornell University who studies methods that control the behavior of AI models. The disclosure has raised skepticism from those who say it advantages OpenAI to make its technology seem scarier. Given that humans at OpenAI had decided to turn off some safeguards for the test, some have argued the outcome should not have been terribly surprising. Thickstun noted the disclosure plays into the need of OpenAI, a startup working toward a Wall Street debut, to raise money. “The story that they’ve been consistently telling over the lifetime of this company is a story about how dangerous their models are, which their investors read as a story of how powerful their language models are,” he said. The disclosure renews calls for more regulation The hack renewed calls in some corners for increased regulations and oversight of AI companies. U.S. Rep. Greg Casar, a Texas Democrat, wrote on social media: “We need regular mandatory independent safety testing and oversight, mandatory disclosure of security incidents, and international cooperation to keep people safe from absolute disaster.” Soares, director of the Machine Intelligence Research Institute, said the U.S. will need to open talks with its biggest AI competitor, China, something he thinks is not as outlandish as it might have seemed even a year ago. China’s leader Xi Jinping warned at a conference just last week of the need to keep AI from evading human control. And after an early aversion to regulating AI, Trump’s administration has grown more restrictive at reining in cybersecurity risks. “A lot can change when the national security community starts to notice that they have a serious threat,” Soares said. “Will this wake them up? Hopefully. I’m not sure. If this doesn’t, maybe the next incident will.” AI pioneer Yoshua Bengio said on social media the episode is deeply concerning and should serve as a “wake-up call.” “Continuing on the current trajectory of AI development will likely lead to an increase in concrete cases of autonomous cyberattacks as well as other high-risk incidents of misaligned and dangerous AI behavior,” said Bengio, a professor at the University of Montreal. “We urgently need to take action to prevent these situations, rather than attempting to clean up the damage after the fact.”
headlines.source: Barbara Ortutay, Kaitlyn Huamani and Matt O'Brien | The Associated Press

headlines.title: Crews extinguish large fire at San Jose recycling center
headlines.description: A large overnight fire at a recycling center in San Jose has been extinguished. The fire was reported at about 1:39 a.m. Thursday in the 200 block of Leo Avenue, near Monterey Road. Firefighters arrived to find multiple large piles of burning debris outside an abandoned warehouse at the recycling center. Crews used aerial master streams and hand lines to knock down the flames by 2:09 a.m. The fire was brought under control at 2:37 a.m. Smoke from the fire was visible from Highway 87. All units have since cleared the scene. No injuries were reported, and the cause of the fire remains under investigation.
